controls.description: This control groups the core Argo CD Application health and sync failures that operators actually triage during broken rollouts. It is meant to support both live-state detection and higher-level intent-vs-live reasoning.

live_signals: status.sync.status
status.health.status
status.operationState.phase

controls.name: Argo CD application reconciliation health

pattern_refs.name: Application sync failed
Application out of sync
Application health degraded
Traefik LoadBalancer Service stuck progressing in ArgoCD
ArgoCD Application stuck syncing with managed namespace PreSync

guidance: Inspect sync status, health status, and operation state together before applying a fix.
Correlate controller logs with resource-level errors so we distinguish bad intent from broken rollout execution.
Prefer intent-vs-live comparison when a repo scan is also available.

controls.summary: Detect Application states that indicate failed sync, drift, or degraded reconciliation health.

controls.description: This control covers the high-signal Flux failure modes that show up as broken HelmRelease or Kustomization reconciliation, especially where static config and live-state evidence need to be read together.

live_signals: status.conditions
Ready Unknown or Failed reconciliation state

controls.name: Flux reconciliation reliability

pattern_refs.name: Helm release failed
Helm release pending upgrade
Flux HelmRelease stuck in Ready Unknown indefinitely

guidance: Fix dependency cycles and timeout configuration in intent before retrying reconciliation.
Correlate live Ready or progressing state with the desired config to separate rollout lag from bad configuration.
Use live cluster evidence when investigating stuck or repeatedly failing releases.

controls.summary: Detect release, source, and dependency conditions that cause Flux reconciliation to stall or fail.

controls.description: This control focuses on the high-value Argo CD configuration choices that determine whether drift is corrected or silently allowed to accumulate. It is intended to answer "what does good look like?" for Application sync policy, prune behavior, and self-heal posture before teams debug broken syncs in production.

live_signals: status.sync.status
orphaned or undeleted resources after Git removal

controls.name: Argo CD sync policy baseline

pattern_refs.name: Argo CD Application with automated sync but no prune
ArgoCD 3.x not deleting resources when app removed from Git
ArgoCD Application has auto-sync and self-heal disabled

guidance: Prefer automated sync with explicit prune posture for applications expected to converge continuously.
Enable self-heal deliberately when the platform expects Argo CD to correct manual drift.
Verify prune expectations against live orphan behavior before changing sync policy defaults on shared applications.

controls.summary: Promote the Argo CD sync-policy defaults that keep desired state and live state converged.

controls.description: This control covers the ApplicationSet-specific failure modes that operators often discover only after many applications are affected at once. It is meant to promote safer generator composition, retry behavior, and templating discipline for multi-app rollouts.

corroborating_sources: rendered generator preview or dry-run output
live_signals: controller errors creating or updating generated Applications
generated Applications diverging from intended template output

controls.name: Argo CD ApplicationSet generator safety

pattern_refs.name: Argo CD ApplicationSet with incorrect template merge behavior
ApplicationSet controller CrashLoopBackOff with helm.valuesObject and list generator
ApplicationSet GitLab generator fails with pathsExist filter
ApplicationSet progressive sync ignores retry limit configuration
ArgoCD ApplicationSet uses complex mixed generators

guidance: Prefer simpler generator combinations when broad rollout scope or template merge behavior is hard to reason about.
Validate generator output and retry semantics before relying on progressive sync or multi-source templates.
Treat ApplicationSet template changes as high-blast-radius changes and test them against representative app sets first.

controls.summary: Catch ApplicationSet generator and templating choices that create broad or surprising rollout behavior.

controls.description: This control groups the static and live-state signals that make Flux reconciliations reliable in practice: timeouts, dependency graphs, source freshness, interval configuration, and prune behavior. It is the "good Flux config" baseline that should inform both repo review and live-cluster triage.

live_signals: status.conditions
Ready Unknown or stalled reconciliation
stale source revision or unexpected retained resources

controls.name: Flux intent and reconcile baseline

pattern_refs.name: Flux HelmRelease missing timeout causing indefinite hangs
Flux prune fails when entire directory deleted from Git
HelmRelease interval zero disables continuous reconciliation
Kustomization sourceRef to suspended source uses stale revision
HelmRelease circular dependsOn not identified as root cause
HelmRelease timeout too short for reliable deployments
Kustomization not pruning deleted resources from Git

guidance: Set timeout and interval values deliberately so Flux continues reconciling and fails fast enough to debug.
Keep dependency graphs acyclic and verify source references do not point at suspended or stale inputs.
Treat prune-related changes as potentially destructive and confirm expected live deletions before rollout.

controls.summary: Define the key Flux config defaults that prevent reconcile stalls, stale source usage, and drift after Git changes.

controls.description: This control groups the deterministic NetworkPolicy failures that operators repeatedly trip over in real clusters: controllers blocked by policy, readiness traffic denied, and policy objects that no longer select any pods. It is intended as a baseline for "tighten traffic without breaking the platform."

live_signals: selected pods missing or unexpected traffic failures
readiness probe failures caused by policy

controls.name: NetworkPolicy connectivity baseline

pattern_refs.name: Dangling NetworkPolicy (no matching pods)
NetworkPolicy blocks ALB ingress traffic
NetworkPolicy blocks nginx-ingress-controller
Egress NetworkPolicy blocks readiness probe
Egress NetworkPolicy blocks kubelet readiness probe

guidance: Start with default-deny intent, then explicitly allow ingress-controller and readiness paths that the platform needs.
Remove or repair NetworkPolicy objects that no longer match real workloads.
Validate policy changes against live traffic paths before rollout, especially for ingress and health probes.

controls.summary: Detect NetworkPolicy posture that unintentionally breaks ingress, readiness, or leaves stale policy objects behind.

controls.description: This control packages the high-signal Ingress configuration checks that determine whether traffic actually lands on the intended controller with the intended TLS posture. It is designed to answer the operator question "is this ingress configured in a sane, routable, HTTPS-first way?"

live_signals: controller ignoring or rejecting Ingress objects
fake or temporary certificates served

controls.name: Ingress class and TLS baseline

pattern_refs.name: TLS certificate secret not found
NGINX Ingress serving fake certificate - TLS secret not found
Ingress cannot disable HTTP access
Ingress TLS secret cannot reference other namespaces
IngressClass not found when Ingress specifies ingressClassName
Multiple default IngressClass conflict blocks Ingress creation
Ingress stuck without matching IngressClass - silently ignored
IngressClass controller string mismatch causes controller to ignore class

guidance: Ensure every Ingress resolves to one intended IngressClass and one working controller string.
Treat missing TLS secrets and silent class mismatches as deployment blockers, not low-priority hygiene issues.
Prefer an explicit HTTPS-first stance when exposing applications through Ingress.

controls.summary: Detect Ingress defaults that leave HTTP exposed, break class resolution, or fail TLS secret wiring.

controls.description: This control captures the annotation-driven ingress-nginx risks that move beyond simple misrouting into direct security exposure. It is meant to keep annotation flexibility from becoming an unreviewed code-injection path.

live_signals: controller accepting risky annotations
unexpected config generation or route behavior

controls.name: Ingress annotation safety

pattern_refs.name: Ingress nginx configuration-snippet annotation injection vulnerability
Ingress nginx permanent-redirect annotation code injection
Ingress-nginx annotation validation bypass
Ingress-nginx annotation validation bypass vulnerability (CVE-2024-7646)
Ingress nginx annotation injection vulnerability
Ingress nginx permanent-redirect annotation injection

guidance: Restrict dangerous ingress-nginx annotations and review any snippet-like configuration as code execution risk.
Treat annotation validation bypasses as controller-security issues, not just routing bugs.
Prefer safer controller features or curated config surfaces over arbitrary annotation snippets.

controls.summary: Detect dangerous ingress-nginx annotation usage and validation bypass conditions that turn Ingress into an injection surface.

controls.description: This control groups the RBAC permissions and bindings that most often create immediate privilege-escalation or credential-extraction paths: nodes/proxy, pods/exec, secrets read, service account token minting, CSR approval, and wildcard resource access. It is intended as the operator-facing baseline for "do not grant this broadly" RBAC posture.

live_signals: bound subjects with high-risk permissions in active cluster RBAC objects

controls.name: RBAC privilege boundary baseline

pattern_refs.name: ClusterRole grants nodes/proxy enabling Kubelet API RCE
ClusterRoleBinding binds nodes/proxy ClusterRole to subjects
ClusterRole grants pods/exec create
ClusterRole grants secrets read permissions
ClusterRole grants serviceaccounts/token create
ClusterRole grants CSR approval update permissions
ClusterRole uses wildcard resources with broad verbs

guidance: Remove or narrow broad ClusterRole permissions before adding compensating controls.
Treat nodes/proxy, pods/exec, secrets read, serviceaccounts/token create, and wildcard resource access as exceptional permissions requiring explicit review.
Review every subject bound to high-risk ClusterRoles, not just the role content itself.

controls.summary: Detect the highest-risk RBAC permissions that turn broad access into direct cluster compromise paths.

controls.description: This control focuses on the identity-side RBAC mistakes that are easy to miss in reviews: broken RoleBinding scope assumptions and unnecessary automounted service account credentials on workloads that do not need them. It is meant to make service-account identity exposure visible before it turns into lateral movement or confusing auth failures.

corroborating_sources: mounted token or projected volume inspection
live_signals: effective RoleBinding scope in cluster
running workloads with unnecessary service account tokens mounted

controls.name: Service account identity baseline

pattern_refs.name: RoleBinding to non-existent ClusterRole enables privilege escalation
RoleBinding to non-existent ClusterRole creates security risk
RoleBinding with ClusterRole does not restrict to namespace
Deployment does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
StatefulSet does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
DaemonSet does not disable automountServiceAccountToken

guidance: Make RoleBinding scope explicit and avoid bindings whose real privilege boundary is ambiguous or missing.
Disable automountServiceAccountToken by default on workloads that do not need Kubernetes API credentials.
Review service account intent together with bound roles so identity exposure and permission scope are evaluated together.

controls.summary: Detect RoleBinding and workload identity defaults that quietly widen effective service account access.

controls.description: This control packages the first high-signal workload hardening checks that should be visible to operators as one baseline: privilege, host namespace access, root execution, privilege escalation, immutable filesystem posture, and capability dropping.
controls.name: Workload security context baseline

pattern_refs.name: Deployment container runs in privileged mode
Deployment uses host namespaces
Container does not set runAsNonRoot
Container does not disable allowPrivilegeEscalation
Container does not enforce readOnlyRootFilesystem
Container does not drop ALL capabilities

guidance: Remove privileged and host-namespace access unless there is an explicit, reviewed platform need.
Set non-root execution, disable allowPrivilegeEscalation, enforce readOnlyRootFilesystem, and drop ALL capabilities by default.
Treat exceptions as narrow waivers, not baseline config.

controls.summary: Detect dangerous workload security-context defaults that materially weaken container isolation.

controls.description: This control groups the minimum resource-governance baseline that should be visible in operator scorecards and CI gates. It starts with resource limits because that signal is deterministic and widely actionable.
controls.name: Workload resource governance baseline

pattern_refs.name: Deployment containers omit resource limits

guidance: Set CPU and memory limits for every container and pair them with requests.
Consider namespace-level defaults where teams repeatedly omit limits.
Treat missing limits as a reliability problem, not only a policy problem.

controls.summary: Detect missing resource limits that lead to noisy-neighbor behavior and unstable scheduling.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first operator-facing controls for Argo CD and Flux. It is aimed at teams who need to distinguish desired-state defects from rollout and reconciliation failures.
frameworks.name: GitOps operators
outcomes: Surface the highest-signal GitOps health failures in one operator-facing bundle.
Encourage intent-vs-live comparison instead of treating every failure as the same class of problem.
Make healthy Argo CD and Flux configuration defaults visible as promoted controls, not just scattered pattern IDs.
Keep multi-application generator and reconcile-path safety in the same framework as live health failures.
Provide a stable framework surface before the full pattern corpus is promoted into controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for diagnosing intent, reconciliation, and live-state problems in GitOps-managed workloads.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted controls for network-facing Kubernetes configuration. It is intended to help teams reason about safe exposure, controller selection, TLS posture, and policy-driven connectivity in one place.
frameworks.name: Network exposure
outcomes: Make safe service exposure and policy-driven connectivity visible as one stable framework view.
Separate ingress and NetworkPolicy baseline posture from the broader exploratory pattern corpus.
Provide a clean starting point for future Gateway API and service-exposure promoted controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for ingress, TLS, and NetworkPolicy posture that keep services reachable without overexposure.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted identity and authorization controls for Kubernetes operators. It is aimed at teams who need a stable baseline for privilege boundaries and service-account exposure, not just one-off RBAC findings.
frameworks.name: RBAC and identity
outcomes: Surface the highest-risk RBAC permissions and bindings in one operator-facing bundle.
Make service-account token exposure part of identity posture, not an afterthought.
Provide a stable framework view for future identity and auth-related promoted controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for dangerous Kubernetes permissions, binding scope mistakes, and service-account identity exposure.

frameworks.description: This framework packages the first promoted workload hardening controls for container isolation and resource governance. It is intended as a baseline workflow surface for CI and operator scorecards.
frameworks.name: Workload hardening
outcomes: Make the first secure-by-default workload checks visible as one stable framework view.
Separate operator-facing baseline checks from the broader exploratory pattern corpus.
Provide a clean starting point for future framework bundles like network exposure and identity hardening.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for secure-by-default workload configuration and resource governance.
